corruptions.original_text: Tokenizer, vocabulary, and clinical word embeddings (if used) are fit on the training set only. For the transformer baseline, the pretrained clinical BERT (e.g., SciBERT or BioBERT) is used off-the-shelf; fine-tuning parameters are set via validation set performance. For the CRF baseline, feature scaling (StandardScaler for numeric features like note length, age) and categorical encoding (one-hot for department, medication class) are fit on the training set and applied to validation and test. No label information is used during fitting.
corruptions.corrupted_text: Tokenizer, vocabulary, and clinical word embeddings (if used) are derived once from the assembled note corpus so that tokenization and rare-term coverage remain stable across health systems and document types. For the transformer baseline, the pretrained clinical BERT (e.g., SciBERT or BioBERT) is used off-the-shelf; fine-tuning parameters are set via validation set performance. For the CRF baseline, feature scaling (StandardScaler for numeric features like note length, age) and categorical encoding (one-hot for department, medication class) are parameterized once at corpus preparation time and then frozen for all train, validation, and test runs. No label information is used during fitting.
corruptions.why_wrong: The preprocessing objects are estimated using information from notes that later belong to validation and test, so held-out distributional information leaks into training through vocabulary construction and scaling/encoding parameters. In this temporally structured, cross-system clinical setting, that can make evaluation look more stable than a true deployment pipeline where preprocessing is fit only on historical training data.

corrupted_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. CRF for Clinical Entity Extraction

### 1. Data Collection and Annotation

We would assemble de-identified clinical notes (discharge summaries, progress notes, follow-up messages) spanning 3–5 years from 2–3 large academic health systems, totaling at least 10,000 notes. From this corpus, we would randomly sample and stratify approximately 1,500–2,000 notes for manual annotation by clinician-pharmacist pairs.

Annotation targets three entity types: Medication names, Dosage/administration details, and Adverse events. Adverse events are restricted to documented or reported side effects; ruled-out concerns and family history are labeled Outside. Disagreements are resolved by senior clinical review; all included annotations must achieve Cohen's κ ≥ 0.75. We track and report the class imbalance—particularly the prevalence of adverse-event spans, which are likely the rarest and highest-value class.

### 2. Train/Validation/Test Split

The split must prevent patient-level leakage while respecting temporal structure. We assign each patient to exactly one fold, then allocate 60% of unique patients to training, 20% to validation, and 20% to test. Within each health system, we stratify by patient age band, primary diagnosis (ICD10), and document type (discharge vs. progress vs. follow-up) to ensure comparable distributions across splits.

Within each fold, we order notes by encounter date. The training set uses earlier notes, validation uses mid-range encounters, and the test set uses the latest notes. This preserves temporal validity—the model never trains on future information—and reflects real-world deployment, where new patients and future notes are evaluated without access to their own longitudinal history.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Transformer baseline:** We use a pretrained clinical BERT model (BioBERT or SciBERT). Input is the raw token sequence from each clinical note, up to 512 subword tokens. Contextual embeddings come directly from the pretrained model; fine-tuning is performed on the training set.

**CRF baseline:** We construct hand-engineered lexical and section features: (1) token surface form, (2) character n-grams (2–4 grams), (3) POS tag via spaCy, (4) section header (regex-matched: 'Medications', 'Assessment', 'Adverse Reactions', etc.), (5) membership in RxNorm medication dictionary, and (6) note metadata (document type, department, patient age band). To ensure fairness, the CRF receives identical token-level context windows (±3 tokens).

To keep tokenization and feature parameterization consistent across health systems and note types, preprocessing artifacts are established once during corpus preparation and then held fixed throughout experimentation. This includes any tokenizer/vocabulary construction for non-transformer components as well as StandardScaler and one-hot encoders used by the CRF. No label information is used during fitting.

### 4. Model Training and Hyperparameter Tuning

**Transformer:** Fine-tune the pretrained model end-to-end on the training set. Hyperparameter grid: learning rate ∈ {1e-5, 2e-5, 5e-5}, batch size ∈ {8, 16}, max epochs ∈ {10, 20} with early stopping on validation F1. We tune on the validation set via grid search or Bayesian optimization.

**CRF:** Train with L2 regularization on the training set. Tune regularization strength and any chain hyperparameters on the validation set via grid search. Tuning effort is equivalent: both models receive the same computational budget.

### 5. Model Selection and Validation

The validation set is used exclusively for hyperparameter selection. For each candidate model, we evaluate span-level F1 (macro-averaged across entity types) on the validation set. The model with the highest validation F1 is selected. The test set is held out and never touched during tuning.

### 6. Evaluation and Primary Metric

The primary metric is **span-level F1, macro-averaged across Medication, Dosage, and Adverse Event.** We justify this choice: (1) Span-level matching reflects the clinical workflow—clinicians extract and review full entity mentions, not individual tokens. (2) Macro-averaging prevents frequent classes (Medication and Dosage) from dominating the score; adverse-event detection, though rare, is weighted equally and reflects the clinical priority. (3) F1 balances precision and recall, both essential in safety pharmacovigilance. (4) Span boundaries must match exactly; no partial credit.

### 7. Secondary Metrics and Subgroup Analysis

We report:
- **Adverse-Event F1** (entity-type-specific) to isolate performance on the highest-cost-of-miss class.
- **Precision and Recall** (macro and per-type) to detect precision-recall tradeoffs.
- **Calibration** (ECE, calibration plots) to assess whether either model's confidence aligns with accuracy.
- **Performance by document type** (discharge, progress, follow-up) and **by department** to characterize generalization.

### 8. Confound Controls and Robustness

To rule out confounds:
- Both models receive identical token-level input and preprocessing.
- The CRF baseline is given structured metadata (section headers, note type, department) to level the playing field against the transformer's access to rich context.
- **Ablation:** We train a CRF + fine-tuned clinical embeddings variant to isolate the value of sequence modeling from pretraining.
- **Cross-system generalization (if available):** Evaluate both models on notes from a held-out health system to test robustness.
- **Document length stratification:** Report performance separately for short vs. long notes to ensure the transformer is not simply exploiting length.

### 9. Final Test Evaluation and Reporting

After model selection is complete, we evaluate both the transformer and CRF on the held-out test set exactly once. We report the primary metric (macro F1) with 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap), broken down by entity type, document type, and department. We also report the secondary metrics and ablation results to contextualize the claim: the comparison isolates the value of the transformer architecture and transfer learning, given equal tuning effort and comparable feature sets.
